content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions month

## 1. Looking for more “adult weird women” movies

**Score:** 325
**Body:** I’m in a spiral right now and wanna keep it going. I’ve already watched:

Secretary (2002)

May (2002)

Muriel’s Wedding (1994)

Dinner in America (2020)

Any other recommendations? Like off putting weird women

older release years are preferred, but really, any year works.

Edit: I’m watching all of your recommendations and will be replying lol!! Thank you so much!!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Harold and Maude
2. the greatest weird women documentary of all time: Grey Gardens. 

And apparently there’s a Drew Barrymore movie based on the true story but I’ve never seen it!
  - The documentary is great. The Drew Barrymore movie is pretty good if you can’t see the doc
3. Romy and Michelle’s hs reunion
4. Wanda (1970), female director 

A Real Young Girl (1976), female director 

A Nos Amours (1983)

The Green Ray (1986) *not off-putting, but she can be a lot 

The Piano Teacher (2001)

Movern Callar (2002), female director 

Me You and Everyone We Know (2005), female director, *not off-putting, but odd

Young Adult (2011)

Blue Jasmine (2013)

Always Shine (2016), female director 

The Favourite (2018)

Tar (2022)
5. Amélie
6. Strictly Ballroom? I thought of this one because you mentioned Muriel’s Wedding which reminded me. It’s one of Baz Luhrmann’s early work. 

She-Devil. 

Carrie.
7. The Piano
8. I Shot Andy Warhol (1996)
9. Ghostworld

## 2. Tremors (1990) is such a fun and highly entertaining movie.

**Score:** 264
**Body:** • Chemistry between all of the cast​ is outstanding. The movie has a lot of funny  moments and dialogues.

• Graboids are genuinely scary, pretty fast , and decently smart too. I like the monsters very much.

• There are also several exhilarating moments, like roof top scene and escape scene.

• I loved the slightly mad couple who literally had fuckton tools and and weapons.

• The soundtrack is also great and movie doesn't leave any wasting space.

**Imo, its a solid 10/10 watch.**  ​

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tremors is a lot of fun. I'm adding that to my rewatch list. Probably haven't seen it for fifteen years? 
2. Some people describe it as a “perfect” movie.

Every scene sets up something that pays off later. No line of dialogue is wasted. Practical effects are stellar. Even the opening foreshadows the climax at the end. Incredibly tight script.

A good example is Chang’s refrigeration pump being broken, and how that pays off later because the main characters hadn’t fixed it yet.

3. What kind of fuse is that?

Cannon fuse.

What the hell do you use it for?

My cannon.
4. When I was a kid sometimes I would start thinking of tremors when I was on my way across the yard or getting out of the car and I would have to make a mad dash for the house. I was so scared lol.
5. I work from home in my basement. A few years ago we had a "powerful for NJ" earthquake. I immediately joked it's the Tremors monsters. We watched 1, 2, and 3 that weekend.
6. Love. It's a great creature horror that I never hear about
7. We’re not handymen. We’re handy men. RIP Fred Ward.
8. The dude‎, Michael Gross, with the weapons was from Family Ties, his character was literally a hippie who had changed with the times, so him being a gun nut goes against type. The wife was famous as well, not sure what for. 
  - Reba McEntire. The queen of country.
9. Saw it in the theater as a teenager. It's a B movie that is actually prestige.

## 3. Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon (2000) is such a piece of art that everyone should watch atleast once in their lifetime.

**Score:** 199
**Body:** This movie is honestly a masterpiece that everyone should watch at least once in their life. I love it so much that I end up watching it again every year.

The movie is blend of action, romance, and drama. The romance, without any obscenity, is outstanding. By the end of the movie, it can easily make you emotional and even bring tears to your eyes. The cinematography is also amazing and makes many scenes feel special.

It’s also a great watch for action fans. I personally like action movies, and this one truly impressed me. The choreography and the way weapons are used make the action scenes very engaging. The movie has many action sequences that are really enjoyable.

Every character has a meaningful story and that’s what turns this movie into such a memorable experience.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One of my favorites for sure.
  - Do you have watched other Chinese movies? I love Chinese classics, like Hero, House of flying daggers, Once upon a time in china, etc. 
Journey to the west really surprised me and it's climax which is face off of Monkey King and Buddha is pure masterpiece. 
    - I’ll check out Journey to the west. I’d recommend fist of Legend with Jet Li. If you haven’t seen “Kung Fu Hustle”. Stop what you are doing immediately and watch that.
2. Ang Lee had such a crazy run in that time period. Ice Storm is one of my favorite movies from that period and I wish it got a little more love. Crouching Tiger is such a love letter to Chinese folklore and cinema and I feel like it’s a must watch for lovers of film. Ideally on the big screen.
3. I remember seeing this in a theater and just being absolutely dumbstruck afterwards.  Totally speechless.  One the most magical theater experiences of my lifetime.
4. I enjoyed it but I've spoken to so many people who found it boring, incomprehensible, or simply couldn't suspend their disbelief for all the flying leaps. It's an incredible film if you are familiar with the cinematic traditions it's drawing from.
  - True. Even my brother didn’t like the movie because of the floating effects and martial arts action, but I found it absolutely amazing. It actually starts off slow and simple, but keeps getting better with every passing minute.


5. One of my favorite movies,beautifully shot and great soundtrack.
6. [deleted]
7. It's a great film. It works equally well for lovers of cinema that want something with artistic merit, and movie fans that just want to see cool shit. 

I miss that era of eastern cinema. Hero, and House of Flying Daggers, amongst others, were excellent as well.

## 4. Asking for the funniest movies of all time, please!

**Score:** 183
**Body:** I absolutely love to laugh, and I was hoping you could share the funniest films of all time. Any era is on the table, from "Duck Soup" to "The Naked Gun" reboot. The bottom line: It has to pass the guffaw test.

Thanks so much for your recommendations, it means a lot!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Popstar Never Stop Stopping
2. Office Space
3. Not a movie, but the six episodes of "Police Squad!" are jam-packed with sight gags.
4. Best in Show
5. Team America
6. Young Frankenstein
7. Planes, Trains and Automobiles
8. A Fish Called Wanda
9. Airplane
  - Airplane? Surely you can't be serious?

## 5. Suggest me a 10/10 movie where no one dies

**Score:** 170
**Body:** Majority of the great movies that I've seen rely on someone's death as a plot point or for driving the climax, and I'm tired of that. Suggest me an utterly beautiful/kickass/amazing movie that doesn't rely on death, where the writing/screenplay itself is great enough to make it a 10/10 film. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Spirited Away
2. Groundhog Day
  - One person dies!
3. Ocean’s Eleven
  - My fav of all time watched over a 100 times.
4. The Martian. One of my favourite films, there's not even an antagonist. And spoiler alert, no deaths.
5. Can people already be dead or come back to life? 


Muppet Christmas Carol 
6. Spotlight

When Harry Met Sally

Victor/Victoria

My Cousin Vinny (there is an off screen murder of someone we meet for 10 seconds, and mention of the death penalty taking place, but it’s such a lighthearted, funny comedy, I think you’d be okay)
  - Can’t believe you’re the only person to mention Spotlight. Absolute 10/10 film.
7. Bridesmaids

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic month

## 1. Rest in peace.!

**Score:** 22241

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His portrayal of a retiring cop on his last day on the job in Falling Down was amazing.
2. Big fan, loved him in the godfather. 95 is a decent innings!
3. Underrated as hell in Gone in 60 Seconds. He’s having so much fun in that movie, you can tell. RIP legend
4. ![gif](giphy|3o7btMBGqquGTVJiF2|downsized)
5. Lonesome Dove. Go watch it.
6. Dude this year blows
  - this year does blow, but a great actor passing away at 95 is not a tragedy.  Living a life that rich and long is a win.
7. A great actor and person!  I first remember seeing him in the movie "Bullitt" as a cab driver back in 1968!  
  - Boo Radley in "To Kill A Mockingbird ".
8. RIP, one of the all time greats and a personal favorite of mine. Just watched open range this weekend.

## 2. Jason Isaacs in The Patriot is one of the most despicable movie villains ever

**Score:** 15851
**Body:** Rewatched The Patriot and I cannot believe how much I hate his character.

Just merciless and a tyrant. Jason Isaacs absolutely crushed it in the role.

Without a doubt, he’s gotta be one of the most hated villains of all time. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tell me about… *Ohio.*
  - Only Jason Isaacs could deliver a line like that and make it sound menacing. 
  - Every Michigander hears that line and thinks,

"Oh fucking wow, he really is fucking terrible!"
2. Agreed. That guy is great in everything he's been in. 
  - He absolutely needs to be a Bond villain. 
  - https://i.redd.it/88xbv6clcqng1.gif


3. Yeah he was great. My buddies and I saw it in theaters. Absolutely hated his character. He was also great in death of Stalin as general Zhukov. 
Edit :not stukov that was the StarCraft general. Haha my bad. 
4. Stupid boy.
5. https://i.redd.it/oom2xtgdspng1.gif
6. He is horrid. Jason Isaacs has some of the most piercing eyes and in this movie you can just tell he has ill intentions from his gaze. He is so cruel. His death is so gratifying. I love this movie.

## 3. This is the reunion we've been waiting for

**Score:** 10266

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cindy, the TV's leaking - Still lives rent free in my head
  - "This bitch is messing up my floors" 
    - I love in the first (third*) one when Cindy is talking to ghost face (brenda*) and he says “I know you did last summer” 

“It was Mardi Gras and I was out of beads!!”

Edit: oops. Had the movie wrong.
    - "Oh my god, Brenda! We're gonna die"
"Well, it just would've been you if you would've just shut the fuck up!"
  - I love "You know your teacher Mrs. Brenda?"

"Yeah-"

"SHE'S DEAD! GONE FOREVER! DIED A HORRIBLE, PAINFUL DEATH! GONE, GONE, GONE JUST LIKE YOUR DOG!"

"My dog's dead?!"

"I JUST RAN HIM OVER WITH THE CAR WHEN I DROVE IN! EVERYONE YOU LOVE AROUND YOU'S DYING!"
2. And we don't question Brenda's immortality 
  - I was going to say, it's been a while since I've seen the movies but hasn't she died at least twice? 
3. goddesses. sashimi. Toyota. Mitsubishi
4. Brenda is the funniest in these movies.
5. The screenshot made me realize I'm still not over my crush on Anna Faris

## 4. This movie is absolute chaos, and the misunderstanding-based humor makes it so entertaining. What do you think about Tucker and Dale vs. Evil?

**Score:** 7964
**Body:** I genuinely believe it’s a hidden gem, and it gave me one of the most fun experiences I’ve had watching a movie. There were zero dull moments; it was fun from start to finish.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “You must think I’m some kind of moron to believe a story like that.”

“No, no sir not at all just….open-minded?”
  - I love that in their conversation Tucker says it would be ridiculous to say this to a cop, then the cop arrives and this is basically line for line what he tells him
    - We have had a REAL DOOZY of a day!
2. I love when the kid runs into the wood chipper and the guy is like “You okay?!”
  - He’s heavy for half a guy. THE STORE HAD BETTER NOT CHARGE ME FOR THIS.
3. WE GOT YOUR FRIEND 
  - OH MY GOD THEY HAVE SARAH!
4. https://i.redd.it/6qd14qexznlg1.gif
5. It’s a masterpiece.
6. Whenever people ask for a good Horror Comedy.

This is usually my recommendation.

So good, Alan Tudyk is amazing as always.

## 5. Which movie moments wouldn’t work nowadays because social media/smartphones?

**Score:** 6957
**Body:** Every time I rewatch The Parent Trap (1998) I think about this scene, if there was social media and phones Annie would’ve easily showed Halle Meredith’s Instagram or whatever and she would’ve recognized her immediately. Idk. Maybe a dumb post but there’s a lot of scenes that wouldn’t fly today. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kevin being abandoned in Home Alone would never have happened (or would have been less likely) with smartphones and social media.
  - and you just know that family would have a ring camera too
2. Many modern movies ignore the existence of social media and/or smartphones because of this.
  - There’s a movie called *Leap Year*, with Amy Adams, that’s basically a remake of *I Know Where I’m Going,* a classic movie from the 40’s starring Wendy Hiller.  

Point is, a cell phone would solve every problem in the original movie.  So in order to make their remake, they had to specifically write a scene in the movie where Amy Adams’ phone couldn’t charge and she was stuck without it.  I found it really funny in that sort of meta way.  They set out writing a remake and quickly realized “oh, the problems from 1945 don’t quite work today, we need to fudge this a bit.”
3. Spiderman 2 when he is unmasked stopping the train. Everyone would have posted his face all over social media and his identity blown
  - Joey Diaz ordered them all to put their fucking phones away
4. Harold and Kumar would have just doordashed and stayed inside
  - "We come too far" is probably the most iconic line of that movie for me

(When they briefly consider going back to the apartment for their phones)
5. https://preview.redd.it/1l4b5s17iqkg1.jpeg?width=1495&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=4db81e2a90df5f7b24244978b9aa6a675d167fcf

In Commando Arnold has to go through an extreme amount of effort to keep a henchman from reaching a phone booth to call his boss.
6. Like 90% of horror movies.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm month

## 1. Kill Bill is a metaphor for grooming

**Score:** 718
**Body:** I have just rewatched Kill Bill 1 and 2 back to back and I feel that the films can be viewed as someone growing out of a grooming relationship. 

The Bride clearly had lingering feelings for Bill when she was rehearsing ber wedding, which is common for people who were groomed. She even called him "daddy" as a double entendre before heading to the aisle. 

She was literally named Kiddo but we were led to believe that Bill was calling her this as a pet name (which also could be a nod to shows like SATC, which were booming at the time, and where the main relationship was between an older man calling his girlfriend in her thirties "kid"). 

Also, Beatrix is framed as "special", Bill has a sweet side that only she knows, Beatrix is the most lacking in backstory and development, she has no past unlike O-Ren Ishii and no future - not until she kills Bill. 

Beatrix is the only one that is not allowed to leave Bill peacefully, all the other Vipers go and do their own thing after the hit at the chapel. 

Their thing is better (O-Ren) or worse (Budd), but it is absolutely theirs. Beatrix does not even have a name until the last half of the second movie. She "roars and rampages" in the film's present, but in the past she was mesmerized by Bill and docile. He treated her terribly and she was all smiles and ready for more. 

She was a "natural-born killer" because he told her that she was, just like he was trying to instill in their daughter. Who was she before? No one. Because Bill made sure that she was no one. 

She only gets her oneness through confronting her groomer - and before that, taking apart the world he had created. Interestingly, Elle, who seems like the biggest Bill devout, turns around and kills Pai Mei. 

She was enamored with Bill, but she wasn't as compliant, she was still her own person; Elle probably despises Beatrix because she is the "favorite", the one who was successfully groomed to be Bill's partner, but being a favorite also means being a puppet until you break your strings violently. 

  
Just needed to get these thoughts out, valuable or not. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. We learn that Bill grew up with a Pimp in Mexico whom he has seen as a father. From him I guess he learned how to manipulate vulnerable women, make them fall in love with him and make them do anything for him - including killing people.
  - I really appreciated that scene; particularly the way Esteban is supposedly being all charming and avuncular and then gives that line “he shot you in the head??? I would have been so much nicer, I would have just cut your face,” which is timed out like a joke, but then one of his girls brings them tequila and we see her face is cut and he grimaces at her then has her wipe her spit up with his hanky and she *thanks* him. 

Just before we meet Bill, and all of his supposed charm; its like the final sign at the gate “abandon all hope ye who enter” that these are men who will control and hurt women around them without conscience or remorse, or honor and humanity, whatever the case may be.

Edit: well, hey, just saw an OC posted earlier to this that actually outlines this but with deeper analysis.
    - My personal head cannon is Beatrix kills Esteban. What a disgusting old man.
2. You can even see how Bill treats Elle Driver in a creepy way: like any woman he is involved with is under his thrall. I wouldn't say this is even a metaphor. Bill \*did\* groom Beatrix, he's a controlling bastard. That's why he's the leader of the vipers
  - Yeah what’s the metaphor? It’s the plot.
  - Code Name: The Snake Charmer
  - The very first shot of Bill in Part 1 is when he calls Elle on the phone to cancel the assassination he ordered, and all you see of him is [his hand stroking and manipulating the phallic handle of his katana. (It has a demon on it too.)](https://youtu.be/ts4gADntnOY?si=9zpz0npQ7tCC-qkW&t=68)
3. I don't think Kill Bill is necessarily a metaphor for grooming more than grooming is just implied within the narrative and an ancillary fuel to the firey revenge. That's just my opinion, though.

I like that you brought it up, because I haven't actually seen a discussion about this before. The analysis in general is really cool and I hadn't thought of a lot of the stuff you did.

Nice post, OP!
4. I really like this read on the film. We know that their relationship was built on violence and an imbalance of power and there are those awkward sexual tension vibes an a way that almost feels taboo when they interact. A lot is being left unsaid but I think you have a really interesting take and I wonder how heavily Tarantino meant to imply this. It does seem subtle yet deliberate
5. I'm grateful to this post because it gives me a different way to think about one of the most important scenes in the films, Beatrix's meeting with Esteban the pimp. Esteban serves as a foil to Bill because he belongs to a different genre than the rest of the film, with a more grounded depiction of misogynistic violence. Rather than killing Beatrix in revenge for running away, he says he "would have just cut your face"—something we immediately see he's done to one of the women he traffics. 

In the past, I've interpreted this scene as clarifying the ethics Bill, Beatrix, and the other characters (even Elle) live by in their heightened revenge tragedy. They may all be murdering bastards, they may all be cruel and duplicitous, they may leave orphans in their wake. But even so, they respect each other at some base human level as worthy adversaries. In Tarantino's universe, it may well be less of an ethical breach to massacre your ex and her wedding guests because she dumped you than it is to mutilate a woman's face to disempower and humiliate her. (And I could say a lot more about the way Tarantino finds contextual justifications for extreme violence against his female characters as not “just” misogynistic, especially in his last two movies…)

Considering your analysis, though, maybe Bill has more in common with Esteban than I thought. And maybe Beatrix has more in common with Esteban's victim, bearing internal scars from being reshaped in Bill's image and having her future family stolen by him. 

## 2. There should be a “recommended point in your life” rating for movies. This can help prevent you from watching films prematurely, and maximize your enjoyment and appreciation of the directors vision and message.

**Score:** 520
**Body:** My father was quite a cinephile growing up. This lead to him exposing me to quite a few classics from a young age. And while I greatly appreciate his desire to educate me, I also regret that I saw some movies way too early. Many movies, I now in hindsight wish I could see again with fresher more mature eyes. 

Sadly, watching these movies older, all while knowing how the plot unfolds really deprived you from capitalizing on your own experiences to truly immerse yourself in what you’re watching. 

On top of that, it might tarnish your original impressions of said film just because you might lack the emotional maturity, or life experiences to relate to the characters. 

Obviously I’m not telling people to wait until they lose a family member to watch Manchester by the sea, but I genuinely wish I hadn’t seen many movies until now. The big lebowski for example I watched when I was 17 and I think that was a huge mistake. 

I believe some of the criterion for my new rating system that could be used are:

Watch when you’ve started stagnating in life and seek purpose. 

Watch when you’ve had children

Watch when you’ve had to care for an adult 

Watch after you’ve experienced love, heartbreak and healing. 

I’d love to hear what other metrics you’d consider in your personal rating system. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A couple of contrary thoughts: I have never once thought it was a mistake to have seen something early and then years later with a new understanding. Those are the moments that give you the best insight into how much you have changed, they are very important. Plus to me, films are meant to be rewatched and lived with; the first watch isn’t all that significant and grows increasingly less so with time.
  - One of the most pleasant and surprising experiences is watching movies as a child and seeing it through the eyes of one of the young characters. Then rewatching as an adult and seeing it through the eyes of the older characters. Especially films with a child-parent dynamic. 

And also my favorite Lost In Translation (2003). For years I saw myself as Scarlett Johansson's character, with Bill Murray's character emotional turmoil as a little bit alien to me. Then one day, 20 years later, re-watching and I'm him, looking at ScarJo like the way I look back at my younger self.
    - Those little scenes with Bill’s wife calling about carpet colors and home decor questions are relatable now. I overlooked them until recently. Those little details strengthen his character’s fall into the abyss of a boring job, international travel demands, seeking adventure, and Scarlett’s pull. 

Similarly, I watched Fincher’s The Game recently. I hadn’t seen it in a long time. The protagonist leads a wealthy workaholic lifestyle until his world is turned upside down. I see the movie in its literal sense and as a perspective of someone experiencing loss of control and no time to grieve. He is on a roller coaster of extreme events, and they are relentless. Completely empathize with his character now as an adult just trying to hold on.
  - I just typed a *way too long* comment to share the sentiment you expressed much more succinctly here!
2. I don’t believe that films are ruined once you’ve seen them because you know how the plot unfolds. For me, only the first viewing is about the plot. Everything else about appreciating the art is for rewatches, which means there’s nothing wrong with revisiting films later in life.
  - agreed. And indeed, watching a movie you haven’t seen since you were young *is still* often like watching an entirely new movie.
3. My father tried to get me to sit through Lawrence of Arabia on video when I was a kid and I thought it was the most boring thing I had ever seen. 

Skip ahead 40 years and I asked my father if he'd like to see the newly remastered version on the big screen.  I 100% expected it would be a labor of love on my part and I'd be able to get a nap in.

Turns out he was right and I was captivated, because by that time I'd seen enough movies to recognize it was a masterpiece.
  - A fucking masterpiece is what it is. It's got the best cinematography. And in this house Lawrence of Arabia is a hero. End of story.
4. This appeal for some "pure" viewing experience, or dismay that your original viewing will be "tarnished" feel like very silly things to waste your energy on.

No two experiences of watching a film will ever be the same, does one ultimate experience tarnish all the others?

Re-watching a film through a new set of eyes has always been exciting for me.  It gives me an appreciation for where I once was and where I am now.

There's nothing wrong with reflecting on your growth.
5. I do agree with you, I watched films when I was too young that went over my head. The great thing is though many films speak to you as a teenager then you find new meanings in when your older on a rewatch. 



## 3. The 'controversy' over 'Three Billboards Outside of Ebbington, Missouri' is because of people taking things too literally

**Score:** 437
**Body:** Spoilers, obvs, for a film that came out 9 years ago. 



I'll be honest, literalism is my biggest gripe over the last 15 years about films across the board. But this film I actually stayed away from because I heard it basically redeemed a really racist, horrible cop character, just to make the audience (esp white audience) feel good about themselves. That made it sound trite and boring to me. 

But here I was with a sick day saturday to myself, and it was on a streamer, so i watched it. 

It was one of the most devastating portraits of grief and anger that I've ever seen. It reminded me of Blue Ruin and Revanche to name a couple others. Mildred is sort of your hero at the start--who wouldn't feel bad and want to root for a grieving mother?--but as the film goes on you see how her grief has turned her into someone that hurts others a lot, including her surviving son. I wasn't 'rooting' for her in the way I root for like, the main character in Get Out to escape the racist cannibals. I felt for her, and hoped she would find peace. 

Then there's Sam Rockwell's Jason Dixon. Just a complete scumbag, who isn't even good at his job. It's not like he's a great detective, he is just also racist and violent. No, he's kind of a terrible cop, too. 

This is where, though, I was waiting for this cheesy redemption arc. And it never came. 

Yes, he tries to solve the murder, but... he fails? and he does it in a boneheaded, stupid way? 

Then he and Mildred set out to murder that soldier who also busted up Mildred's store earlier. And yeah, maybe he raped and burned a girl while serving overseas but... we don't know that, they don't know that. Even he did, though: wasn't the whole thing we went through in 2020 about restorative justice, abolition of prisoners, and defunding the police, about how cops shouldn't be the judge jury and executioner? 

And if you don't think Martin McDonagh, the filmmaker KNEW this, then you haven't read or seen his other works. McDonagh is the anti-Tarantino. He makes violent movies and plays to show how freaking futile it is to spend your life on revenge. Just like the teenage bride says (from her bookmark about 'polio or polo' lol) anger begets greater anger. It doesn't mean Mildred is 'bad', no, a great film shouldn't be caught up in labeling someone good or bad, so the audience can pat themselves on the back for being 'one of the good ones'. It certainly doesn't make Dixon 'good', cuz he definitely isn't! I'd argue that going to Idaho to murder a guy is just more of the same for him! 

A redemptive arc for Dixon would be, like, going to therapy, and then becoming a therapist himself or something. Not committing vigilante justice. 

So this film was incredible for me, and it wasn't always fun to watch, because of how tragic it was. But I'm glad I finally saw it. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I didn’t love this movie (it was okay) but I always thought this argument against it was so weak. We were not supposed to walk away from it thinking Sam Rockwells character was a hero or redeemed or anything like that. I question the critical thinking skills of anyone who came away from the movie thinking that was what it was trying to make them think. 
  - Yea, the whole point is learning to live together in the shadow of problems you are incapable of fixing. Rockwell’s character ends the story still an awful, violent, crooked cop. The world is full of people like him, and you can’t wish them away. But sometimes, you can come to an understanding with them.

It’s a bit of a shame for me, because it’s got a fantastic premise and themes, an A+ cast, a lot of powerful moments, and so many interesting ideas.  I think it just got let down by the details.
2. Luckily I wrote something about this in 2018, so I can accurately remember what I thought about a movie I haven't seen since then. :)

Most of the complaints seem to center around the character of Dixon and whether he is "redeemed" at the end. I think this criticism not only misses the point of the ending but reveals that the critics of this point might be unaware of what really offends them about it.

The movie isn't about Dixon and his arc, it's about Mildred and her arc. She begins as a righteously angry grieving mother fighting 'the man' with a bold public move, and ends in a morally dubious plan, partnering with a violent racist creep. In this way, the movie can be read as a scathing critique of the Justice Warrior and a warning about how easy it is for self-righteously blinding rage to morph into something unsavory, counter-productive and quite the opposite of how it was intended. I feel like what many of the critics of the politics of the movie are reacting to is how it reflects on themselves, "the good guys," rather than how it muddies its portrait of "the bad guys."  
  
At the end, with rage leading the line, the difference between "good guys" and "bad guys" is neglible.
  - > The movie isn't about Dixon and his arc, it's about Mildred and her arc.

I think it is both, but we only really realize it when going back to re-watch and clearly see their starting and ending points.

Otherwise I very much agree with the rest of your write-up. Mildred starts the story in a very morally defensible and sympathetic situation and ends up making violent and angry choices. And because of that, her end point intersects with the end point of a violent and hateful cop who ends up making choices to be a bit more patient and understanding.  And we see Dixon at his lowest dealing with grief by attacking Red at the advertising office and Mildred at her lowest dealing with grief by burning down the police station. But then Dixon improves and Mildred gets worse following their low points, culminating in their meeting at the middle where neither are 'good'. 

Dixon's arc is interesting because early on you aren't expecting that by the end of the movie you are hoping for him to succeed as he makes better choices (gathering evidence at the bar) and Mildred's is interesting because her cause is so righteous that you get a bit blindsided by how counterproductive her decisions become.


We do also get some examples of 'good' choices from Red, the new chief, and Peter Dinklage's character, and 'bad' choices from Mildred's ex husband and the soldier. So we see the contrasting trajectories of Dixon and Mildred in those contexts too so that we are reminded that they are both at all times capable of deciding the kind of person they want to be.
3. The moral panic around the film and Rockwell’s character was so overblown and overwrought that it truly felt like satire at the time for anyone with even a half-working brain lmao

I cannot fathom how you could watch that film, let alone understand Mcdonagh as a writer/filmmaker, and come away with the idea that he is glorying or supporting the actions of an abusive police system.

It was just low-information viewers hopping on the nebulous concept of “police bad” to pat themselves on the back about their sociopolitical anxieties at the time.

Three Billboards was and is a fantastic film in the catalogue of a fantastic filmmaker.
4. Three Billboards…is a movie that I’m glad that I saw, parts are deeply moving and stayed with me for a long time. But McDonagh doesn’t know a damn thing about American life and it hurts the film; the characters and situations don’t have the cultural specificity of his works set in Ireland.

I’m thinking of the Black friend and employee of Frances McDormand’s character. She gets arrested on a drug possession charge to intimidate Mildred, then, after several days, gets released and happily returns to work, a smile on her face. It’s not really a character at all, just someone who exists in the film to be the object of another character’s racism.
  - The dialogue doesn’t fit either. It makes sense for In Bruges but is jarring and out of place for this setting
5. The thing with controversies like this is they become oversimplified over time. I think there are absolutely issues with how he wrote about racism in this film (in large part because it's something the film focuses a lot on, but primarily just to add texture to characters/setting), but to boil it down to "the movie is bad because it's about a racist cop's redemption" is beyond silly. I think to really explore this topic you'd have to read some critical essays exploring this, and not just go off of the impressions you got off social media. Honestly it kind of reminds me of the One Battle After Another discourse (a movie that I much prefer but is also not beyond criticism).
6. I always thought the film made it very clear that Sam Rockwell's character wasn't redeemed by the end of the film. But he might have started on the road to redemption. Personally I think on the road, the two characters would get sidetracked, and eventually decide against committing vigilante justice. They're getting to the place they need to be but they aren't there yet.
7. My interpretation: that soldier was absolutely the murderer. The whole movie has themes about people overlooking the flaws of their own people, and taking steps to protect their people regardless of right/wrong or legality. So the army officer guy claiming his solider was overseas when the cops call asking is just one more example of this theme. He would know what his soldier was capable of, so naturally he wouldn’t hand his guy over. 

We already know the soldier had a sure connection to Mildred and the daughter’s story, otherwise why’d he come to the store and wreck stuff?

I have zero doubt at all that the soldier did it, and I don’t think it was even ambiguous. The only question in the end is whether Mildred and Dixon will go through with killing the soldier. And I think they do.

The movies not about redemption, it’s about human tribalism, and Dixon has found his new tribe. Just my own interpretation. Personally I loved it.

## 4. Marty Supreme: A largely mediocre film that keeps meandering.

**Score:** 433
**Body:** So I just watched Marty Supreme and have some thoughts. Personally thought it was a mediocre 3/5 movie that isn't as good and profound as the hype suggests. It's a movie about the banality of ambition for ambition's sake based around a character who is more of a sniveling grifter than someone actually obsessed with the game. Marty's more obsessed with the idea of his own greatness than he is with ping pong. His dream ends up being a nothing burger at the end and he ultimately has to settle down for the very thing he resented: a mediocre, mundane life with responsibility towards family.

All very good but I really wish the movie tried to present this in a better way. I think it falters after he returns from the first ping pong trip and the dog plotline ensues, which I think served no purpose other than trying to show us that Odessa A'zion's character would do anything for Marty and to keep him with herself. I personally didn't think that was necessary and the conceit of her character was well portrayed with the makeup injury scene anyways.

And Gwyneth Paltrow served no other purpose than to maybe provide some meta commentary on the art of method acting while playing a washed up actress being a washed up actress herself (no disrespect to her). But really what is the point of Marty sleeping with her? To show us that he is a deadbeat grifting scoundrel with no sense of responsibility? I think that could have been shown in a better way in his interactions with Kevin O'Leary's character. You can also see that in his interactions with Tyler The Creator's character.

Also I interpreted the ending as a failure on Marty's part. I have myself been in similar situations where ambition is so extreme that settling for anything less doesn't feel right. I am not convinced that Marty Mauser will not abandon his child for another impulsive ping pong spree to Japan. His tears read more like despair at the loss of his dream than an acceptance of his life's new, very banal and mundane direction. And I also don't think Marty is a bad person. He is a hustler and is a product of his environment, background and upbringing. It doesn't feel good to settle for less. Some people try their hardest to achieve more than what they have been given in life and that doesn't make them a bad person. I sympathized a lot with Marty's character and constantly kept wishing that he was smart enough to achieve it. But he was not, a point the movie conveys very well. The entire point of his character: that he is not smart enough.  I just really wish Josh Safdie stayed true to the character.

Ultimately I think the film just meanders trying to make a spectacle more than doing anything substantial. The entire movie felt like a subplot. I really wish there was more nuance to Marty's character. He felt more like a caricature of his character's archetype than a real person. Anyways Timothée Chalamet is good as a sniveler. At his most charismatic in one of his least charismatic roles. And I think that's all I have to say.

Edit: LOL how did this post blow up all of a sudden?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't think the movie is particularly deep, but I do think the action, editing, acting, cinematography, and most importantly for me the music make it a delightful thrill ride of a movie. I'm absolutely fine with movies that are just spectacle if the spectacle is inventive and engaging. OPN's releases this year (even his contributions to the Weeknd soundtrack) have been excellent.
  - The music put me off sooooo much. This takes place in the 50s and no one really looks or talks or acts like someone from that time period, but I can forgive that because the acting is so good. However the vaporwave soundtrack broke any immersion it had left. The choice to set the film in 1952 is almost inconsequential and redundant. You could take that script, set it in the early 80s, and it would have zero effect on the film, actually I'd argue it would make it better.
2. I think this reads more like moral frustration than criticism. Describing Marty as a “snivelling grifter” or Paltrow as a “washed up actress” closes things down too quickly. Once the characters are reduced to types, there isn’t much room left for ambiguity, or for the possibility that the film’s roughness might be deliberate.

If Marty is more in love with the idea of greatness than with ping pong itself, that feels less like a flaw than the point. The film seems interested in ambition detached from craft — striving as performance. The chaos isn’t evidence that he’s listless; it’s part of the predicament he’s created. He lives inside instability because that’s the only space where the fantasy can survive.

The structure reflects that. The story lurches because he lurches. His drive is real, but it isn’t disciplined. That’s different from drift.

I also read the ending differently. He wins on his own terms, and in the scene with his nemesis — the “vampire” bit before the match — he laughs it off and performs. That suggests he isn’t only self-mythology. When it matters, he can deliver.

After that, choosing a normal life doesn’t look like defeat so much as proportion. He tests the dream and reaches its limit. That feels less like surrender and more like recognition.

The disagreement may just be about what counts as success. If you’re looking for transcendence, the film will seem thin. I’m more drawn to the bungled and the botched
  - Agree, this was a good analysis
  - fwiw i have been in competitive gaming communities and that kind of 'negotiating with success' is really common for people who were shooting for becoming #1. first you wannt be the GOAT, then #1 for the year, then maybe i wanna win a few tournaments, then maybe it's enough for me to win just one, then maybe it's enough to just beat the best player in the world so that i know, in my heart, if my life was different, i could've done it all.

I get that Josh Safdie wants to create endless, frantic energy but the way he did it here felt highly contrived, to the degree that whatever point the film was trying to make about ambition or parenthood or discovering pasts felt like it was actually bringing down everything I did like about it. 

It's stunning to look at, the soundtrack (both original and jukebox) was exquisite, and all of the performances were top-notch. When I got to the end, I have to believe there's something darker under the surface there because no way did the preceding film make me believe or feel that Marty Mauser was on the way to being reformed man because he won an informal ping-pong match and saw 'his' child.
4. i think the point of the paltrow character is that she represents the kind of life marty feels like he should be leading. like, he believes movie stars should want to have sex with him. and he's right! he makes it happen! he kind of misses that she's bored and depressed and not doing well in life in general, because all he can see is a big neon sign over her head that says "SUCCESS" and that's all he wants. 

I'll add that I also found it really odd how the film picks up after the first tournament. i guess it's a comment on how no matter what you achieve, it's kind of meaningless to your little circle of family, who can only see you in the role you've had your whole life. but for a *movie* it just dramatically was odd to have him have an adventure and success and smash cut he's back in the kitchen, he's broke, he's hustling, as if it never happened.

5. Here's what I think, and pardon me if I ramble a bit but I don't think the movie meanders, even though I also might. It tells a story of a young guy who thinks he knows what means most in life, so he pursues it, come hell or high water, and get his ass smacked (literally and metaphorically) as a result. 


The problem I had with the movie (specifically the storytelling) was that there were so many supporting characters that just didn't get an ending in the film. Wally essentially loses his taxi cab, and then is never heard from again, Kay has that moment after the review comes in and we never hear about her career again, so on and so forth. I'm personally not a fan of when a movie introduces incredibly important characters that contribute massively to the main character's story and then just forgets about them.


However, I'm thinking maybe the point of the movie was to show how (per Marty's own words) he had to make sacrifices towards his ambition. And how, in life -even though the movie exaggerates it a lot- we sometimes have to forgo* closure for progress, i.e. the final scene when he sees his baby, and how badly ambition can damage you and the people around you. I believe he was crying, not because he was accepting the mundane, but because he knew he was giving up his dream for something he had been fighting against the whole time and was finally giving in because he realized he wanted to. 


I think the point of the movie was to convey that life doesn't always tie up neatly in a knot and many times we leave frayed ends behind. Hell I should know, I've left enough of those to last me a lifetime. I think the point was to convey (in 2h20m) how messy life can get, even if you aren't some skeezball like Marty is, and even if you have real talent, pursuing that talent to the extreme can be dangerous to those you care about. And ambition is something that should be taken with caution. I'd potentially compare it to The Wolf of Wall Street, where that same ambition turned him into a hound who was also a complete mess. 


While I don't think the movie ended gracefully due to a bunch of characters basically being forgotten, I still think it tells a very poignant cautionary tale about how dangerous ambition can be to your own personal sphere. How you have to tread lightly with ambition because it can turn into desperation and greed so easily. I mean, shit the movie kinda foreshadows it with the actor throwing the chair out the window and then saying "you said I should shoot for the stars!" during the play. 


I think, ultimately it's not an AMAZING movie, but it's actually more solid than a lot of comments here are saying, it certainly has flaws, the pacing felt... Well like a rollercoaster, but then again it was a drama not a thriller, so a bit of up and down is not bad I suppose. And I think it told a solid story, I just wish we would've gotten more to know what happened to everybody else


God I didn't think I'd write this long of a comment but I guess I enjoyed the movie enough to say something so... 🤷

## 5. I just finished Mulholland Drive and genuinely have no idea what I just watched

**Score:** 363
**Body:** I finally watched Mulholland Drive last night because it’s always on those “greatest movies ever made” lists. I went in expecting a complicated mystery… but I genuinely feel like my brain got scrambled.

For the first hour or so, I thought I understood what was happening. Then the story just kept getting stranger and stranger, and by the end I wasn’t even sure what parts were real, what parts were dreams, or if any of it actually happened.

Some things that completely lost me:

The whole shift in identities near the end

The blue box / blue key situation

Club Silencio (which felt important but I have no idea why)

Whether the first half of the movie is supposed to be a dream

I’m not even saying it’s bad, it was actually really fascinating and creepy but I feel like I’m missing something huge.

So for people who understand this movie…

What is actually going on?

Is there a generally accepted interpretation, or is it intentionally meant to be confusing?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Someone could probably explain this a lot better than me but I'll give it a quick go from what I remember:

Diane (Naomi Watts) and Camilla (Laura Harring) are aspiring actresses who had a sexual relationship with each other that meant a lot more to Diane than it did to Camilla. Eventually Camilla started to become successful and she essentially dropped Diane for the affections of Adam (Justin Theroux), a famous director. Diane became depressed and jealous and hired the hitman to kill Camilla, who told her that the job would be confirmed once she received a blue key. Upon seeing the key, she became guilty with what she had done and was driven to suicide.

Now, that's all covered in the second half of the movie, but the first half is the dream that Diane has in her dying moments, in which all of the details of her reality are moved around to create a more pleasant picture of her life in review. In this world, she's a much more charming and promising young actress named Betty who kills it in her audition and secures the undivided love of a clueless Camilla (aka Rita). Also, the arrogant Adam is a cuck, the hitman can't get his job done, and Diane Selwyn is just a random woman whose dead body they come across, eliminating all of her other real-life concerns. Some of the other details of the dream are more mysterious and unresolved, like the dream of the man in the diner and club silencio, but they can be seen as abstract hints to the lies and ugliness that Diane is  keeping from herself in this state.

In her dream, nasty truths like the meaning of the blue key are turned into fun little plot devices that you would see in a traditional mystery movie, while her record of being cheated on, traumatized, and eventually dying an unglorious death are projected onto other people. Essentially it helps her to have the classic yet unattainable "Hollywood Dream" that she had always wanted just before she dies, simply by being able to shift some stuff around.
  - Agree with all this except that the dream does not take place during her dying moments. At the start we see a POV of a head hitting a pillow, that’s Diane in a depressed state going to sleep. When the Cowboy says ‘time to wake up’ and we hear knocking sounds, that’s when Diane wakes up, because in reality her lesbian ex roommate is knocking on her front door.

She spends the day moping around her house, wracked with depression and guilt over murdering Camilla, and at the end she goes completely insane and blows her brains out.
    - Yeah, the first two hours are the dream (lol the pillow shot) and the last half hour explains the reality. It’s kind of like a reverse Wizard of Oz.
  - You nailed pretty much everything, just that the dream doesn't start in her dying moments but right before it. After the beginning swing dance scene it cuts to a blurry pov shot of someone in bed turning their head into the pillow and fading to black, which is Diane going to sleep and the fantasy dream starting. Then at the end when she wakes up is when she finds the key delivered to her on the table, starts fantasising about their relationship and sex, gets overcome with guilt which then manifests in that old couple chasing her before killing herself. 

And I think Diane Selwyn's corpse in the dream, since that's her name in reality, symbolises both her guilt and her impending doom because of it. As well as a kind of omen or foreshadowing that the dream is coming to an end due to her 'skeletons in the closet'. Which is also a similar thing I think the homeless man jump scare represents—the filthy, repressed guilt.

It's been a while but I think those are the sequence of events. Also, I had this thought just the other day about the Club Silencio scene—there's a lot of meta and surreal stuff going on with illusions and seeing through them. Perhaps it was a real event they both went to, and the 'NO HAY BANDA' performance helped Diane realise the illusion of her romanticised 'Hollywood dream' relationship with Camilla. And this is why she seizures and snaps out of the dream when reexperiencing it.
2. Another thing i would like to add is that for me Mulholland Drive feels like a sister movie to Lost Highway. Both have similar "dissociated state" like structure and similar themes, but from different perspectives. 
  - i'm happy to see somebody else saying this, it's something i been thinking about for like twenty years. two different treatments of the same idea of how do you live with yourself after doing something terrible to somebody, you either invent an entire fantasy world to live in until it falls apart around you, or you physically become a different person
3. I think approaching David Lynch films with "whodunnit" approach is kinda wrong approach to take. Sure, there are some quite plausible solving-the-puzzle explanations you can find online, but IMHO that's not the point of his films. Enjoying magical experience, subconscious mindfucks and having personal explanation is much more worthy and meaningful then having "what is actually going on" explained to you.
  - I watched this film for the first time when i was about 18 and never had an experience like it before. I was amazed at how i could enjoy a film so much and yet have no idea what was going on, i felt something subliminally.

But i immediately went to the internet to look up explainations and honestly that helped me appreciate the film even further. I dont necessarily see anything wrong with it, it didnt overly intellectualise the film or anything

4. I think I know the answer but the obvious question is if you've ever seen a David Lynch film before. He generally operates in the realm of surrealism, and his films are really things intended to be felt more than understood. Mulholland Drive is very open to interpretation, but the most important plot point to understand is that the first two thirds of the film are a dream, and when the cowboy says "Time to wake up, pretty girl", most if not all things after that are "real". The film fucking rules, I remember when I first saw it, I watched it like 20 times that month to try and figure out what its deal was.

Also some context for the film, it was originally intended to be a TV show. The initial concepts were for a Twin Peaks spin-off focused around the character Audrey, before becoming its own thing. The first two thirds of the film are effectively a pilot, but the TV studios rejected it. Lynch then got funding from Studio Canal to complete the work as a single feature film, so that also explains some of the disjointedness and plot threads that don't actually go anywhere
